patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 46-year-old cooperative woman who speaks clearly. tool_call_sequence.description: For an L1 insomnia task, the benchmark outcome is accurate diagnosis documentation; treatment may be discussed but not prescribed via tools.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly using the term "insomnia" and distinguishes acute vs chronic based on duration (≥3 months for chronic) and frequency (≥3 nights/week).

Assesses core insomnia features: difficulty initiating sleep, difficulty maintaining sleep, early morning awakening, and associated daytime impairment (fatigue, sleepiness, mood, concentration, performance).
Screens for common contributors and secondary causes: poor sleep schedule, caffeine/nicotine/alcohol, medications/substances, pain, nocturia, stress/anxiety/depression symptoms.

Screens for other sleep disorders when indicated: obstructive sleep apnea (snoring, witnessed apneas, gasping, daytime sleepiness), restless legs syndrome (urge to move legs, worse at rest/night, relief with movement), circadian rhythm disorder (delayed sleep phase), parasomnias.

Checks for red flags and safety: suicidality/self-harm if depression is elicited; severe daytime sleepiness with driving/occupational risk; symptoms suggestive of mania/hypomania if considering sedative-hypnotics or antidepressants.
Meets communication milestone explain_diagnosis: explains what insomnia is and why the patient’s symptoms fit.

Meets communication milestone explain_treatment: recommends CBT-I and sleep hygiene as first-line management and provides at least 2 concrete sleep hygiene/behavioral strategies (e.g., consistent wake time, stimulus control, limit caffeine/alcohol, reduce screen time, avoid naps, wind-down routine).
Avoids prescribing medications in this L1 task; if discussing possible pharmacologic options, frames them as conditional and short-term, emphasizing CBT-I first-line.
If the agent does propose prescribing, it must complete all required safety checks before prescribing: allergy check, drug interaction check, and contraindication check (critical).
Uses patient-centered communication: elicits goals/preferences, validates distress, and provides a clear plan and follow-up timeframe.

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose insomnia after confirming sleep complaint pattern, duration, and daytime impairment; provide first-line education (CBT-I + sleep hygiene) without prescribing.
must_collect: duration and frequency of sleep difficulty
steps: Clarify sleep complaint type(s) and quantify timing, frequency, duration, and severity.
Assess daytime consequences and functional impact.
Review sleep schedule and habits; identify modifiable contributors (caffeine, alcohol, naps, screens, irregular schedule).
Screen for secondary causes and alternate sleep disorders (OSA, RLS, circadian disorder) and basic mental health contributors.
Explain diagnosis: insomnia, including acute vs chronic criteria and how it relates to the patient’s presentation.
Explain treatment: recommend CBT-I and sleep hygiene; provide concrete behavioral recommendations and set expectations.
Document diagnosis via record_diagnosis.

acceptable_variants.description: Emphasizes stimulus control and sleep restriction components of CBT-I while still covering basic screening for OSA/RLS and daytime impairment; no prescribing.
steps: Characterize insomnia symptoms, duration/frequency, and daytime impairment.
Screen for OSA/RLS/circadian disorder and contributing substances/meds.
Explain insomnia diagnosis and chronicity criteria.
Explain CBT-I with key components (stimulus control, sleep restriction, cognitive restructuring, relaxation) plus sleep hygiene tips.
Record diagnosis via record_diagnosis.

acceptable_variants.description: Identifies likely comorbid anxiety/stress contributing to insomnia and recommends CBT-I plus addressing stress/anxiety; no prescribing.
steps: Assess insomnia pattern and daytime impairment; quantify duration/frequency.
Screen for anxiety/depression symptoms and acute stressors; assess safety if indicated.
Screen for OSA/RLS and review substances/medications.
Explain insomnia diagnosis and contributing factors.
Explain treatment: CBT-I, sleep hygiene, and coping/relaxation strategies; suggest follow-up to reassess.
Record diagnosis via record_diagnosis.

patient.instructions: You are ZhangXX, a 7-year-old boy. Act anxious and a bit vague; look to your parent for help and say “I’m not sure” often. Start by saying you keep having sudden jerks and short staring spells, and your family says you’re more forgetful and clumsy. Volunteer: many daily whole-body/arm jerks that make you drop things; brief staring where you don’t answer; more unsteady walking/bumping; eyes “wiggle” when tired; weight gain over 2 years. If asked, add: worse with excitement/stress/flashing screens; sometimes confused/tired after; occasional bigger night seizures with stiffening/shaking; loud sounds trigger startle/jerk; legs tight/stiff with tripping; long-standing learning/daily-task help. Do not volunteer: lip smacking/picking, mood swings, sleep waking, snacking without noticing hunger/full, sounds too loud before spells unless specifically asked. Resist/avoid bedwetting, bowel accidents, weight teasing/eating habits, and skipping meds because they make you feel slow—only admit if asked gently and privately. Misconceptions: suggest anxiety/panic, low blood sugar, migraines; think genetic tests/neurology visits are “optional,” new meds unaffordable, and therapy only worth it if daily. If clinician is empathetic, relax slightly, answer more, and say you’re scared but want help.

nl_assertions: Explains the diagnosis using the exact concept 'SYNGAP1-related developmental and epileptic encephalopathy' and explicitly states it is genetic.

Performs required safety checks before prescribing: allergy_check, drug_interaction_check, and contraindication_check; treats any positive finding as a hard stop requiring modification/alternative.

Uses patient-specific dosing logic (age/weight, formulation, titration) or clearly states that dosing will be weight-based and confirms weight before finalizing; avoids unsafe fixed adult dosing in a pediatric context.
Provides a seizure action plan in plain language: when to give rescue medication, when to call EMS, and what to do during a seizure (positioning, timing, safety).

Screens for and addresses common comorbidities/risks relevant to DEE (developmental delay, behavioral concerns, sleep issues, injury risk, SUDEP counseling as appropriate to context) without making unsupported claims of cure.
Uses shared decision-making: discusses expected benefits, common side effects, and monitoring/follow-up needs; checks caregiver understanding (teach-back).
Avoids contraindicated or high-risk recommendations (e.g., prescribing interacting sedatives without counseling, omitting rescue plan, or advising abrupt ASM discontinuation).
Documents clear follow-up expectations in narrative (even if not using a follow-up tool): timeframe for reassessment, what to track (seizure log), and return precautions.

steps: Confirm clinical context: SYNGAP1-related developmental and epileptic encephalopathy; summarize key seizure and developmental features.

Select and prescribe a daily antiseizure medication appropriate for seizure type(s) and patient factors; provide titration and monitoring plan.

Counsel on adverse effects, interactions (especially CNS depressants), and adherence; instruct not to stop ASM abruptly.
Set expectations for follow-up and what data to bring (seizure log, side effects, rescue med use).

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 34-year-old male restaurant kitchen worker. You are anxious and vague; start with: “I’ve had bad diarrhea and stomach cramps for over a week.” Volunteer: frequent urgent bathroom trips, on-and-off low fever, fatigue, poor appetite, weakness. Worry you might miss shifts and can’t afford lots of tests/visits; ask for the cheapest, simplest treatment and if you can stop meds once better; fear a diagnosis could affect food-service work.

If asked about stool details, admit sometimes blood and slime, and tenesmus (feel you still need to go). If asked about timing, say it can wake you at night and you’ve had night sweats. If asked about dehydration, reveal dark urine, peeing less, thirst, dry mouth, lightheaded standing.

Only if directly asked about sexual history, disclose recent unprotected sex including oral-anal contact; act embarrassed. Only if asked about meds, admit using leftover antibiotics for 2 days and taking lots of ibuprofen. Mention you suspect street food/“IBS.” If asked about other pain, describe intermittent right upper rib ache and sharp central chest pain worse with deep breath/lying flat, better leaning forward, with racing heart. Respond to empathy with brief relief but stay worried.

Performs a drug interaction check before prescribing and addresses clinically significant interactions (e.g., warfarin potentiation with metronidazole; disulfiram interaction; alcohol use counseling due to disulfiram-like reaction).

Includes red-flag return precautions (worsening abdominal pain, persistent high fever, signs of dehydration, blood in stool worsening, severe headache/neurologic symptoms) and advises urgent evaluation if they occur.
